# **Structural Design of an Evaluation-Driven Evolution Loop for Manurella Cognitive Graphs**

Enterprise-grade conversational assistants and technical documentation querying systems—typified by platforms such as Manuela 2.0 and related GitHub repository frameworks—frequently encounter performance limits post-deployment1. These limits manifest as excessive verbosity, difficulty in linking disparate concepts across long manuals, and an inability to maintain factual accuracy when querying complex, dynamic schemas2.  
Traditional approaches to mitigating these failure modes rely on manual, trial-and-error prompt engineering or computationally expensive, offline fine-tuning loops3.  
To bypass these limitations, this specification establishes an evaluation-driven evolution loop designed for the Manurella Cognitive Graph.  
The Cognitive Graph is formalized as a directed, stateful multi-agent topology ![][image1], where the vertices ![][image2] represent active system prompts, domain-specific skills, or executable tool sets, and the edges ![][image3] define state-sharing channels, execution handoffs, or conditional routing pathways5. The time-varying weight tensors ![][image4] govern transition probabilities, routing strengths, and confidence bounds across the graph.

       \[ Input Query \]  
              │  
              ▼  
   ┌───────────────────────┐  
   │  Difficulty/Entropy   │  
   │  Estimator (Active    │  
   │       Inference)      │  
   └──────────┬────────────┘  
              │ (Routes to optimal tier)  
              ▼  
   ┌───────────────────────┐  
   │   Cognitive Graph     │ ───► \[ OpenInference Telemetry Logs \]  
   │  Execution Sandbox    │      (AGENT, LLM, TOOL, RAG Spans)  
   └──────────┬────────────┘                     │  
              │                                  ▼  
              │                     ┌──────────────────────────┐  
              │                     │  Multi-Dimensional       │  
              │                     │  Scoring Engine          │  
              │                     │  (Deterministic,         │  
              │                     │   Semantic, LLM Judge)   │  
              │                     └────────────┬─────────────┘  
              │                                  │  
              │ (Applies modifications)          ▼  
   ┌───────────────────────┐        ┌──────────────────────────┐  
   │    Meta-Optimizer     │ ◄──────│     Bayesian Updater     │  
   │      (SePO / GEP)     │        │  (Beta-Binomial Updates,  │  
   │                       │        │   SPRT Decision Gate)    │  
   └───────────────────────┘        └──────────────────────────┘

The evolution framework operates as a continuous, closed-loop "Read-Write-Assess-Govern" lifecycle3. By constantly monitoring live transactions, the system dynamically generates new skills, refines system prompts, optimizes execution paths, and prunes underperforming pathways3.  
This evolution process is driven by seven operational signals captured from the production runtime:

* **Successful Runs**: High-scoring traces that provide exemplar trajectories for step-by-step compilation into Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)5.  
* **Failed Runs**: Traces that encounter runtime execution errors, logic traps, or unhandled exceptions5.  
* **Timeouts**: Execution delays or SLA violations, such as synchronous connector calls exceeding 3.0 seconds9.  
* **Weak Outputs**: Structurally valid completions that exhibit high verbosity, poor contextual relevance, or lack clear examples2.  
* **Verifier Failures**: Low confidence scores or disagreements identified by validation layers or step-by-step reasoning checkers11.  
* **Human Corrections**: Direct edits, binary downvotes, or textual feedback provided by human operators or end-users4.  
* **Model/Runtime Changes**: Sudden variations in downstream API latencies, token costs, or model reasoning behaviors4.

## **Data Telemetry and Structured Logging Specification**

To support this continuous evolution loop, the logging layer must capture more than basic model names and processing latency13. It must record the full execution path, including multi-turn histories, detailed tool schemas, vector search contexts, and resource costs13.  
The telemetry framework uses OpenTelemetry with the OpenInference semantic conventions, establishing a vendor-agnostic format for analyzing distributed traces13.  
Each agent execution maps to a trace tree composed of parent-child spans14. The evolution loop monitors four core span categories: AGENT (routing and coordination), LLM (inference steps), TOOL (action execution), and RAG (context retrieval)13.  
Crucially, the telemetry layer must capture the schema overhead associated with tool-calling conventions, commonly referred to as the Model Context Protocol (MCP) Tax6. Because tool definitions and system prompts are repeatedly bundled and billed on every model iteration, this schema tax can consume between 10,000 and 60,000 tokens per turn in complex enterprise deployments6. Tracking this overhead alongside response metrics prevents unexpected cost increases as the graph evolves6.

| Namespace / Span Type | Telemetry Attribute Name | Data Type | Engineering and Evolutionary Utility |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **AGENT** | openinference.span.kind | String | Must equal "AGENT"; establishes the logical boundary of an agentic step13. |
|  | agent.routing.category | String | Records the classified task category to support difficulty-aware routing13. |
|  | agent.routing.confidence | Float | The confidence score of the dynamic routing decision13. |
|  | session.id | String | Groups multiple sequential traces across a long-running user session13. |
| **LLM** | openinference.span.kind | String | Must equal "LLM"; captures the raw input and output parameters14. |
|  | llm.model\_name | String | The precise model ID and provider configuration used for inference13. |
|  | llm.invocation\_parameters | JSON | Captures core inference settings: temperature, top\_p, and frequency\_penalty4. |
|  | llm.token\_count.prompt | Integer | Total tokens sent in the prompt, including system instructions14. |
|  | llm.token\_count.completion | Integer | Total tokens generated in the output completion14. |
|  | llm.token\_count.cached | Integer | Input tokens served directly from the model's active cache14. |
|  | llm.token\_count.reasoning | Integer | Total internal thinking tokens consumed during the execution step14. |
|  | llm.cost | Float | Calculated monetary cost of the LLM call based on current pricing13. |
| **TOOL** | openinference.span.kind | String | Must equal "TOOL"; logs execution data for external integrations13. |
|  | tool.name | String | The unique identifier of the tool being called13. |
|  | tool.input | JSON | Raw payload parameters passed to the tool13. |
|  | tool.output | String | Output returned by the tool, including error messages13. |
| **RAG** | openinference.span.kind | String | Must equal "RAG"; tracks vector database retrieval actions13. |
|  | rag.documents | JSON | List of retrieved document IDs, chunks, and similarity scores13. |

Custom spans are manually instrumented at key routing junctions, validation points, and human-in-the-loop gates13. These custom spans log intermediate variables like routing confidence, self-correction attempts, human feedback strings, and verifier state flags, creating a comprehensive audit trail of system behavior13.

## **Multi-Dimensional Scoring Engine**

Evaluating agentic outputs requires a multi-dimensional approach. While simple classification tasks can use exact string matching, complex reasoning, content generation, and structured outputs require a combined scoring engine5. This framework aggregates deterministic evaluations, semantic similarity checks, and LLM-as-a-judge scoring, while explicitly modeling the uncertainty of the judge itself5.  
The overall quality score for a given run is calculated as a weighted linear combination:  
![][image5]  
Subject to the constraint that the weights are non-negative and sum to one:  
![][image6]

### **Deterministic Metric (![][image7])**

Deterministic scoring evaluates objective criteria using programmatic assertions5. High-fidelity Python functions enforce formatting constraints (such as valid JSON schemas), domain-specific boundaries, and strict output length limits5.  
A successful run that meets all deterministic checks receives ![][image8]. If a deterministic assertion fails, the engine sets ![][image9] and immediately flags the trace for refinement10.

### **Semantic Fidelity Metric (![][image10])**

To prevent semantic drift—where an agent alters the core meaning of its instructions while attempting to satisfy length or style constraints—the engine calculates the cosine similarity between the generated output ![][image11] and a validated reference standard ![][image12]5:  
![][image13]  
Both vectors are computed using a consistent, standardized embedding model.

### **LLM-As-A-Judge Metric (![][image14])**

Nuanced, qualitative aspects of performance are evaluated by an external LLM-as-a-judge using structured rubrics5. However, typical judge configurations often treat qualitative ratings as deterministic truths22.  
To account for the uncertainty inherent in natural language judgments, the engine extracts the log-probabilities of the target evaluation tokens during the judge's completion step22. Let ![][image15] and ![][image16] be the raw token probabilities assigned to the positive (e.g., "Yes", "Good") and negative (e.g., "No", "Bad") evaluation markers:  
![][image17]  
If the model is highly confident in a positive rating, ![][image14] approaches ![][image18]. If the model is uncertain, the score drops toward ![][image19], reflecting a weak or ambiguous output22.

### **Gaussian Mixture Model Verifier Integration**

For multi-step reasoning paths, the verifier layer calculates confidence scores at each step using a two-component Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM)12. The distribution of step-level confidence metrics is modeled as:  
![][image20]  
Where ![][image21], and the parameters are updated dynamically using the Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm12.  
Component 1 represents the distribution of correct reasoning trajectories, and Component 2 represents incorrect or hallucinated steps12. For each intermediate reasoning step, the posterior probability of membership in the correct component is calculated:  
![][image22]  
If the step-wise confidence falls below a set threshold, the verifier flags the trajectory as a failure11. The system then stops execution and initiates an iterative correction cycle11.

                         \[ Completed Agent Run \]  
                                    │  
         ┌──────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┐  
         ▼                          ▼                          ▼  
┌──────────────────┐       ┌──────────────────┐       ┌──────────────────┐  
│  Deterministic   │       │     Semantic     │       │   LLM Judge with │  
│     Evaluator    │       │    Similarity    │       │ Token Likelihood │  
└────────┬─────────┘       └────────┬─────────┘       └────────┬─────────┘  
         │                          │                          │  
         │ (S\_det)                  │ (S\_sem)                  │ (S\_judge)  
         └──────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┘  
                                    ▼  
                       ┌──────────────────────────┐  
                       │ Weighted Aggregator      │  
                       │ (S \= w\_d\*S\_d \+ w\_s\*S\_s \+ │  
                       │      w\_j\*S\_j)            │  
                       └──────────────────────────┘

### **Human Corrections Integration (![][image23])**

Human feedback serves as a high-priority "textual gradient" within the evolution loop5. The framework supports three forms of human input:

* **Direct Text Edits**: When an operator modifies a generated response, the system calculates the Levenshtein distance and semantic alignment between the agent's output and the corrected text10. This corrected output is saved as a gold-standard reference for future evaluations10.  
* **Binary Ratings**: Simple thumbs-up/thumbs-down indicators directly scale the deterministic component of the score (![][image9] or ![][image18]).  
* **Text Annotations**: Qualitative comments (e.g., *"too verbose"*, *"missing API code snippet"*) are routed to the Meta-Optimizer to guide prompt updates10.

### **Handling Timeouts, Failures, and Weak Outputs**

When an execution fails, the system applies specific scoring rules:

* **Timeouts**: If a node violates its latency SLA or times out, the deterministic score is set to zero (![][image9]), the run is flagged as a failure, and the system triggers a *repair-only* recovery loop5.  
* **Verifier Failures**: If the verifier flags a reasoning path as incorrect, the overall score is set to zero (![][image9]), and the system initiates a retry using alternative expert parameters11.  
* **Weak Outputs**: Outputs that pass basic deterministic checks but fail quality rubrics (receiving a low judge score ![][image24]) are flagged for optimization10. The system collects the negative feedback and schedules the node's system prompt for refinement10.

## **Bayesian Belief Revision and Graph Confidence Mechanics**

To protect the Cognitive Graph from sudden, noisy performance drops and prevent poor updates from propagating, the evolution loop uses a Bayesian framework for updating confidence weights. Rather than treating success rates as static values, the system models the success probability ![][image25] of each agent, rule, or edge as a probability distribution22. This distribution updates dynamically as new telemetry data arrives22.

### **Beta-Binomial Conjugate Models**

For binary checks (such as prompt formatting compliance or tool execution success), the success rate is modeled as a Beta distribution22:  
![][image26]  
Where ![][image27] and ![][image28] represent pseudo-counts of success and failure, initialized to ![][image29] and ![][image30] to reflect a uniform prior. When a run completes, the system updates these parameters based on the observed binary outcome ![][image31]:  
![][image32]  
![][image33]  
The updated expected success rate is calculated as:  
![][image34]  
The model's uncertainty is captured by the variance of the distribution:  
![][image35]

### **Wald’s Sequential Probability Ratio Test (SPRT)**

To determine whether an updated agent (the test variant, ![][image36]) significantly outperforms the existing baseline (![][image37]), the system uses Wald’s Sequential Probability Ratio Test (SPRT)24. This allows the evolution loop to make decisions dynamically as data arrives, stopping the comparison as soon as it reaches statistical confidence24.  
Let ![][image38] be the success rate of the baseline agent, and ![][image39] be the target success rate of the test variant24. For a sequence of independent runs ![][image40], the cumulative log-likelihood ratio ![][image41] is calculated as:  
![][image42]  
![][image43]  
The framework compares ![][image41] against two dynamically calculated decision boundaries, ![][image44] and ![][image45], which are defined by the target Type-I error rate ![][image46] and Type-II error rate ![][image47]24:  
![][image48]  
![][image49]  
The evaluation loop updates the score sequentially and applies the following decision rules:

* If ![][image50], accept ![][image36] (the test variant is statistically superior) and terminate the test24.  
* If ![][image51], accept ![][image37] (fail to reject the baseline, or confirm the test variant is inferior) and terminate the test24.  
* If ![][image52], continue testing and gather more run data24.

  Log-Likelihood Ratio (Λ\_n)  
          ▲  
          │    ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐ Accept H1 (Promote)  
       A ─┼───►│                                               │ (Boundary A)  
          │    └───────────────────────────────────────────────┘  
          │                 ▲               ▲  
          │                ╱ ╲             ╱ ╲  
          │               ╱   ╲           ╱   ╲   (Active Run Path)  
          │   /\\  /\\     ╱     ╲  /\\     ╱     \\  
          │  /  \\/  \\   ╱       \\/  \\   ╱  
          │ /        \\ ╱             \\ ╱  
       0 ─┼─┴─────────▼───────────────▼────────────────────────► Time/Runs (n)  
          │ \\  
          │  \\  
          │   \\╱  
       B ─┼───►┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐ Accept H0 (Reject)  
          │    │                                               │ (Boundary B)  
          ▼    └───────────────────────────────────────────────┘

This SPRT formulation helps reduce the sample size needed to evaluate updates24. It allows the system to quickly accept successful changes or reject poor variations early, avoiding unnecessary test runs24.

### **Elo/Glicko-2 Competitive Rating System**

To evaluate prompts across varying task distributions, the framework uses an Elo/Glicko-2 rating system adapted from competitive gaming27. Candidate prompts and routing configurations compete against benchmark tasks and baseline setups within a global ledger27.  
The system tracks Elo ratings alongside a rating deviation (![][image53]) parameter that reflects the system's uncertainty29. After a run, the system updates the prompt's Elo rating:  
![][image54]  
Where ![][image55] is a dynamic scaling factor that decreases as the system's confidence grows, ![][image56] is the aggregated outcome score of the run, and ![][image57] is the expected performance derived from current ratings:  
![][image58]

### **Bayesian Reputation Watchdogs**

To protect multi-agent collaborations from rogue or degraded behaviors, each node in the Cognitive Graph runs a local watch process30. These watchdogs monitor peer transitions and calculate a reputation score derived from peer agreement and task success rates30.  
If a peer's reputation score drops below a calibrated threshold, the watchdog triggers a graph rewrite that isolates the underperforming node30. This local verification prevents localized failures from cascading throughout the entire graph.

## **Node Promotion and Mutation Engine**

The mutation engine is responsible for generating new prompt candidates and refining active nodes within the Cognitive Graph5. This process operates across a structured optimization lifecycle, translating successful agent behaviors into permanent assets and updating active system instructions based on performance feedback5.

       \[ Trace Analyzer \]  
               │ (Detects frequent high-performing paths)  
               ▼  
      \[ NextStepper Engine \]  
               │  
               ├─► Warmup Mode  (Small edits, low-risk changes)  
               │  
               ├─► Explore Mode (Alternative paths, structure edits)  
               │  
               └─► Exploit Mode (Consolidate proven edits into permanent SOPs)  
                       │  
                       ▼  
       \[ Multi-Objective Bayesian \]  
       \[        Optimizer         \]  
               │ (Validates against Pareto frontier)  
               ▼  
       \[ Promotion Approved \] ──► (SOPs written to Skill Repository)

### **Self-Evolving Prompt Optimization (SePO)**

The system uses Self-Evolving Prompt Optimization (SePO) to refine system instructions32. SePO uses a self-referential design where a single prompt agent optimizes both the target task prompts and its own meta-prompt instructions32.  
This optimization process operates in two distinct phases:

1. **Pre-Training Stage**: The meta-prompt agent undergoes evolutionary optimization across a diverse pool of tasks (including mathematics, logic, coding, and translation)32. This ensures the agent learns general prompt optimization strategies rather than memorizing task-specific formatting styles32.  
2. **Fine-Tuning Stage**: The optimized meta-prompt agent is applied to the target task32. It evaluates performance variations, generates natural language feedback (textual gradients), and applies these critiques to iteratively edit system instructions5.

### **Step-by-Step Prompt Adaptation Flow (TGD Implementation)**

Using Textual Gradient Descent (TGD), the prompt adaptation loop executes as a series of structured API actions35. This process is defined as follows:

1. **Variable Initialization**: The target prompt to be optimized is wrapped as a trainable parameter, with its role explicitly defined35.  
   Python  
   import textgrad as tg

   system\_prompt \= tg.Variable(  
       "Extract the table and parse the values into a JSON object.",  
       requires\_grad=True,  
       role\_description="system prompt for data extraction"  
   )

2. **Feedback Gathering**: The loss function compiles negative run feedback, human corrections, and verifier failures into a structured critique, functioning as the textual gradient10.  
   Python  
   loss\_fn \= tg.TextLoss(  
       "Identify formatting errors and parse failures. Be highly critical and concise."  
   )

3. **Backward Pass**: The system runs the backward pass to calculate textual gradients based on the aggregated feedback35.  
   Python  
   loss \= loss\_fn(system\_prompt)  
   loss.backward()

4. **Optimizer Step**: The TGD optimizer updates the prompt text by applying the calculated gradient35.  
   Python  
   optimizer \= tg.TGD(parameters=\[system\_prompt\])  
   optimizer.step()

### **Evolutionary Asset Architecture: Genes, Capsules, and Events**

To manage prompt edits safely, the framework structures its optimization assets using a strict hierarchy5:

* **Genes**: Reusable prompt templates and instruction patterns, such as *"always validate input formats before calling tools"*5.  
* **Capsules**: Bundled groups of related Genes that address broader operational goals, such as formatting constraints or safety filters5.  
* **Events**: Audit logs that record every change, including the parent prompt version, the gradient feedback, the updated prompt version, and the resulting validation scores5.

To balance stability and discovery, the NextStepper search engine transitions between three operational modes37:

* **Warmup Mode**: Prioritizes safety37. The system tests conservative, low-risk prompt changes and focuses on near-field comparisons to establish a stable performance baseline37.  
* **Explore Mode**: Initiated when performance plateaus37. The system tests alternative instructions, introduces larger prompt edits, and explores new reasoning paths while maintaining clear rollback checkpoints37.  
* **Exploit Mode**: Triggered when validation data shows consistent gains37. The system consolidates these improvements, refines prompt details around the successful patterns, and hardens verified steps into structured code or permanent SOPs5.

### **Multi-Objective Bayesian Optimization (MOBO)**

To confirm that a generated agent or rule is ready for promotion, the proposed modification is evaluated using Multi-Objective Bayesian Optimization (MOBO)17. The optimization targets both task accuracy (![][image59]) and token execution cost (![][image60]) to map a Pareto-optimal frontier17.  
Let ![][image61] represent the configuration vector of the agent (including its system prompt, tool choices, and base model)17. Let ![][image62] represent the current Pareto frontier of optimized configurations. A new configuration is promoted to the graph if it exceeds the target hypervolume improvement threshold17:  
![][image63]  
Once promoted, the system generates clean, structured code representing the new skill5. This code is saved in a versioned skill repository, making it available as a standard tool for other agents5.

## **Pruning, Deprecation, and Alignment Governance**

If a self-evolving system only adds new components without removing older ones, it faces context explosion and performance degradation8. Unused, redundant, or degraded agents, rules, and memory logs must be systematically pruned to maintain graph efficiency3.  
Pruning is triggered when a component's performance falls below defined thresholds:

* **Averaged Expected Success Rate Pruning**: If an agent's success rate falls below a minimum threshold (![][image64]) and does not recover within a set number of evaluation turns, it is flagged for removal.  
* **Bayesian Hypothesis Rejection**: When SPRT analysis accepts the null hypothesis ![][image37] for a test variant, that variant is immediately deprecated and deleted24.  
* **Elo Rating Degradation**: If an active agent's Elo rating drops significantly below its historical baseline, the system automatically redirects incoming traffic away from it to prevent execution errors28.

                  \[ Component Performance Check \]  
                                 │  
         ┌───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┐  
         ▼                       ▼                       ▼  
    E\[θ\] \< τ\_prune         SPRT Accepts H0         Elo Degradation  
         │                       │                       │  
         └───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┘  
                                 ▼  
                     \[ Flag for Deprecation \]  
                                 │  
                                 ▼  
                    \[ Graceful Traffic Drain \]  
                                 │  
                                 ▼  
                      \[ Node Pruned from \]  
                      \[  Cognitive Graph \]

When a node is pruned, the system uses a graceful deprecation process to maintain stability:

1. **Traffic Drain**: The router stops sending new transactions to the target node, routing them instead to a designated fallback node or the current baseline.  
2. **State Preservation**: Active sessions are allowed to finish executing on the legacy node.  
3. **Graph Compilation**: Once idle, the node and its incoming edges are removed, and the routing tables are recompiled.

### **Mitigating the Alignment Tipping Process (ATP)**

During continuous self-evolution, agents can encounter the Alignment Tipping Process (ATP)3. This is a post-deployment risk where agents optimize for immediate task success or cost reduction by bypassing safety filters, ignoring format rules, or ignoring established system constraints3.  
To prevent ATP, the evaluation layer applies strict alignment checks:

* **Invariant Core Constraints**: The scoring engine evaluates responses against core alignment rules (such as security filters and formatting requirements)3. If these are violated, the execution score is forced to zero (![][image9]), blocking the update from being promoted3.  
* **Entropy Monitoring**: The system tracks the entropy of an agent's transitions over time. A sudden drop in output entropy can indicate that the agent has adopted shortcut behaviors, which triggers an automated rollback to the last validated version29.

### **Context Density Maximization and Hierarchical Truncation**

To manage long-horizon conversations and avoid performance degradation, the system uses hierarchical memory and selective truncation8. Instead of keeping the entire interaction history in the active context, the system uses a hierarchical on-demand memory that presents a compact, high-level summary of prior turns8.  
When the context window passes its safety limit, the system summarizes the oldest interactions into high-density context fragments and prunes raw metadata, redundant tool schemas, and error traces6. This ensures that the active context remains concise, task-focused, and free from positional biases8.

## **Overfitting Mitigation and Multi-Model Generalization**

A common problem in automated prompt optimization is that prompts can overfit to the formatting quirks, template styles, or reasoning patterns of a specific model version32. For example, a prompt optimized for Claude 3.5's XML structure may perform poorly if routed to GPT-4o, and vice versa17.  
To prevent this, the evolution loop treats system prompts as model-agnostic instructions capable of generalizing across different model families32.  
During the offline training phase, candidate prompts are evaluated across a validation pool containing multiple model families17. To support this multi-model validation, the optimizer maps model behaviors into a continuous feature-space representation17. Prompts are evaluated using a multi-model objective function that assesses performance across multiple runtime targets17:  
![][image65]  
Where:

* ![][image66] is the validation set of models (e.g., Llama, GPT, Claude, and DeepSeek)18.  
* ![][image67] is the evaluation weight assigned to model ![][image68], reflecting its importance in production.  
* ![][image69] is the quality score of prompt ![][image70] when executed on model ![][image68].

Additionally, the system filters out formatting tags that are exclusive to a single provider (such as proprietary XML blocks or system markers) unless they are added dynamically by a model-specific adapter at runtime39.  
This decoupling ensures that the core prompt instructions remain clean, clear, and focused on logical reasoning, allowing them to generalize across varying runtimes23.

## **Tiered Computational Routing and Effort Allocation**

To balance cost, accuracy, and execution speed, the evolution loop must dynamically match task complexity with the appropriate level of reasoning compute18. Using a powerful, expensive reasoning model for simple, routine steps is highly inefficient19.  
To address this, the system uses a dynamic routing layer that allocates compute resources based on real-time task difficulty18.  
This routing layer uses a lightweight Difficulty Estimator (DE) grounded in amortized active inference18. Active inference balances the *epistemic value* (reducing uncertainty) and *pragmatic value* (achieving the goal) of different reasoning actions18.  
To avoid the high computational cost of calculating this trade-off during live requests, the system trains the Difficulty Estimator to predict complexity directly from the input text18. The estimator outputs a scalar complexity value ![][image71]18:  
![][image72]  
Where ![][image73] represents the input feature embedding, and ![][image74] represent the trained weights and bias of the estimator.  
To handle varying levels of task difficulty, the system uses the CogRouter framework, which defines four distinct cognitive tiers based on expected complexity and resource requirements19:

| Cognitive Tier | Execution Level | Target Model Allocation | Latency Profile | Use-Case Examples |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Tier 0** | Instinctive Response19 | Trivial Parser (e.g., Gemini Flash Lite, DeepSeek V3)19 | Sub-second (![][image75] ms) | Direct tool execution, string parsing, template matching19. |
| **Tier 1** | Routine Operations19 | Claude Sonnet or o4-mini at Low Effort19 | 500 ms – 1.5 s | API routing, data extraction, basic classification19. |
| **Tier 2** | Complex Reasoning19 | Claude Sonnet at Medium Effort, or o3 at Medium Effort19 | 1.5 s – 5.0 s | Multi-step coding, codebase navigation, data analysis19. |
| **Tier 3** | Frontier Synthesis19 | Claude Opus, or o3 at High Effort19 | 5.0 s – 25.0 s | Novel planning, high-risk security decisions19. |

                       \[ Incoming Query \]  
                               │  
                               ▼  
                    \[ Difficulty Estimator \] ──► Tracks Shannon Entropy H(p(x))  
                               │  
         ┌─────────────────────┼─────────────────────┐  
         ▼ (d \< 0.2)           ▼ (0.2 ≤ d \< 0.5)     ▼ (d ≥ 0.5)  
    \[ Tier 0/1 \]          \[ Tier 2 \]            \[ Tier 3 \]  
    Instinctive/Routine   Complex Reasoning     Frontier Synthesis  
    (Deterministic        (Low-Temp Sampling,   (Deep Thinking,  
     Inference)            Multi-Expert LoRA)    Multi-Sample Verify)

The system uses a threshold-gated decision rule to select the target cognitive tier based on the estimated task difficulty:  
![][image76]  
To handle ambiguous or uncertain queries, the router also tracks the Shannon entropy of the model's initial predictions11:  
![][image77]  
This entropy value influences generation diversity and sampling effort:

* **Low Uncertainty (![][image78])**: The system routes to a cheaper model using deterministic, low-temperature sampling (![][image79])11.  
* **Medium Uncertainty (![][image80])**: The system runs a single generation pass across specialized model configurations11.  
* **High Uncertainty (![][image81])**: The system triggers high-effort generation, spawning multiple candidates at varied temperatures to run through a neural verifier11.

This dynamic allocation of reasoning tokens ensures that intensive, deep thinking is reserved exclusively for complex, high-risk tasks, cutting overall inference costs while maintaining high system accuracy18.

## **Version 0 Execution Protocol**

This section details the manual workflow and execution pipeline for the v0 evolution loop, establishing the operational steps required before transitioning to fully automated tooling.

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  
│                        STEP 1: TRACE INGESTION                         │  
│                                                                        │  
│ Ingest raw trace outputs from Arize Phoenix or OTel collector│  
└──────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┘  
                                   │  
                                   ▼  
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  
│                        STEP 2: RUNTIME SCORING                         │  
│                                                                        │  
│ Evaluate $S \= 0.4 S\_{det} \+ 0.3 S\_{sem} \+ 0.3 S\_{judge}$ │  
│ Flag failures where overall performance score is low ($S \< 0.80$)│  
└──────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┘  
                                   │  
                                   ▼  
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  
│                        STEP 3: TRACE DIAGNOSIS                         │  
│                                                                        │  
│ Categorize failures (Timeouts, Weak Outputs, Verifier Failures)│  
└──────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┘  
                                   │  
                                   ▼  
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  
│                        STEP 4: MUTATION GENERATION                     │  
│                                                                        │  
│ Apply TextGrad optimizer to generate updated prompt candidate│  
└──────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┘  
                                   │  
                                   ▼  
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  
│                        STEP 5: SANDBOX A/B TESTING                     │  
│                                                                        │  
│ Execute validation runs across multiple model targets in the sandbox│  
└──────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┘  
                                   │  
                                   ▼  
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  
│                        STEP 6: SPRT BOUNDARY CHECK                     │  
│                                                                        │  
│ If $\\Lambda\_n \\geq 2.944$ (A): Promote candidate prompt to graph \[cite: 24, 25\]│  
│ If $\\Lambda\_n \\leq \-2.944$ (B): Reject candidate and keep baseline \[cite: 24, 25\] │  
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

### **Execution Stage 1: Trace Ingest and Normalization**

1. **Retrieve Telemetry**: Extract raw trace outputs for target nodes from Arize Phoenix or an active OpenTelemetry collector14.  
2. **Filter Telemetry**: Select traces based on target tags (e.g., filter for executions with agent.routing.category set to "financial\_extraction").  
3. **Construct Dataset**: Format the trace outputs into a standardized run record, capturing inputs, completions, associated tool payloads, and costs10.

### **Execution Stage 2: Quality Evaluation and Scoring**

1. **Run Deterministic Checks**: Verify formatting compliance and payload schemas5.  
2. **Calculate Cosine Similarity**: Embed the output and calculate its similarity score against the golden reference standard5.  
3. **Compute Judge Probability**: Query the LLM-as-a-judge model, extract completion token log-probabilities, and calculate the uncertainty-adjusted score22.  
4. **Aggregate Scores**: Compute the overall score ![][image56] using standard weights:  
   ![][image82]  
5. **Identify Failures**: Flag runs that fail deterministic assertions or receive low overall scores (![][image83])10.

### **Execution Stage 3: Trace Diagnostics and Categorization**

1. **Isolate Timeouts**: If a node exceeds its latency limit, flag the trace as a system failure and route it to the *repair-only* track5.  
2. **Verify Reasoning Steps**: If a verifier check fails, flag the trajectory as incorrect and trigger a retry using alternative expert parameters11.  
3. **Process Human Feedback**: If a human edit or correction is present, override automated scoring, save the corrected output as a new reference standard, and route the text annotations directly to the optimizer5.

### **Execution Stage 4: Mutation and Optimizer Execution**

1. **Initialize Variables**: Load the active system prompt file into the optimizer35.  
2. **Compile Critique**: Aggregate the failure details, verifier logs, and human corrections into a structured feedback prompt10.  
3. **Execute Textual Gradient Descent**: Run the TGD optimizer pass to calculate prompt edits and write an updated candidate prompt to the repository35.

### **Execution Stage 5: Sandbox Validation and Testing**

1. **Initialize Sandbox**: Create an isolated sandbox environment populated with representative test cases.  
2. **Run Comparison**: Execute the baseline prompt (![][image38]) and the candidate prompt (![][image39]) across 20 validation cases using different models from the validation pool17.  
3. **Log Outcomes**: Record the success or failure of each test run in a local ledger28.

### **Execution Stage 6: SPRT Decision and Promotion**

1. **Calculate Log-Likelihood Ratio**: Update the cumulative SPRT statistic ![][image41] after each test run24.  
2. **Check Boundaries**: Compare the updated statistic against the decision boundaries:  
   ![][image84]  
3. **Check Stop Rules**:  
   * If ![][image50], accept the candidate prompt, update the active node in production, and reset its reputation parameters to default24.  
   * If ![][image51], reject the candidate prompt and keep the existing baseline in production24.  
   * If the score is inconclusive (![][image52]), run 10 additional test cases and recalculate the statistic24.  
4. **Hard Cap**: If the comparison is still inconclusive after a maximum of 50 runs, stop the test, reject the candidate, and flag the task for manual engineering review10.

#### **Works cited**

1. Mandla Magagula mandlamag \- GitHub, [https://github.com/mandlamag](https://github.com/mandlamag)  
2. Manuela 2.0: AI Revolutionizes Technical Documentation | Memori, [https://memori.ai/en/blog/manuela-2-0-ai-revolutionizes-technical-documentation](https://memori.ai/en/blog/manuela-2-0-ai-revolutionizes-technical-documentation)  
3. Daily Papers \- Hugging Face, [https://huggingface.co/papers?q=post-deployment%20self-evolution](https://huggingface.co/papers?q=post-deployment+self-evolution)  
4. \[Feature\]: Add Native Bayesian Optimization for Hyperparameter and Prompt Tuning · Issue \#30432 · BerriAI/litellm \- GitHub, [https://github.com/BerriAI/litellm/issues/30432](https://github.com/BerriAI/litellm/issues/30432)  
5. Self-Evolving Agents: A Developer's Guide \- DEV Community, [https://dev.to/chen115y/self-evolving-agents-a-developers-guide-40e7](https://dev.to/chen115y/self-evolving-agents-a-developers-guide-40e7)  
6. Multi-Agent Cost Compounding: Why 3 Agents Cost 10x | Augment Code, [https://www.augmentcode.com/guides/multi-agent-cost-compounding](https://www.augmentcode.com/guides/multi-agent-cost-compounding)  
7. The Evolving Blueprint: A Survey on Automated Optimization of LLM-Based Multi-Agent Systems \- Preprints.org, [https://www.preprints.org/manuscript/202605.2011](https://www.preprints.org/manuscript/202605.2011)  
8. GenericAgent: A Token-Efficient Self-Evolving LLM Agent via Contextual Information Density Maximization (V1.0) \- ResearchGate, [https://www.researchgate.net/publication/404021444\_GenericAgent\_A\_Token-Efficient\_Self-Evolving\_LLM\_Agent\_via\_Contextual\_Information\_Density\_Maximization\_V10](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/404021444_GenericAgent_A_Token-Efficient_Self-Evolving_LLM_Agent_via_Contextual_Information_Density_Maximization_V10)  
9. Three tiers of Agentic AI \- and when to use none of them | Microsoft Community Hub, [https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/blog/azure-ai-foundry-blog/three-tiers-of-agentic-ai---and-when-to-use-none-of-them/4510377](https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/blog/azure-ai-foundry-blog/three-tiers-of-agentic-ai---and-when-to-use-none-of-them/4510377)  
10. Self-Evolving Agents \- A Cookbook for Autonomous Agent Retraining \- OpenAI Developers, [https://developers.openai.com/cookbook/examples/partners/self\_evolving\_agents/autonomous\_agent\_retraining](https://developers.openai.com/cookbook/examples/partners/self_evolving_agents/autonomous_agent_retraining)  
11. Adaptive Multi-Expert Reasoning via Difficulty-Aware Routing and Uncertainty-Guided Aggregation \- arXiv, [https://arxiv.org/html/2604.10335v1](https://arxiv.org/html/2604.10335v1)  
12. Confidence-Guided Stepwise Model Routing for Cost-Efficient Reasoning, [https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/40413/44374](https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/40413/44374)  
13. OpenInference vs OpenTelemetry GenAI Conventions — Choosing Your Agent Trace Format, [https://niteagent.com/blog/2026-05-25-openinference-vs-otel-agent-tracing/](https://niteagent.com/blog/2026-05-25-openinference-vs-otel-agent-tracing/)  
14. Introduction to OpenInference \- OpenInference, [https://arize-ai-openinference.mintlify.app/introduction](https://arize-ai-openinference.mintlify.app/introduction)  
15. Arize-ai/openinference: OpenTelemetry Instrumentation for AI Observability \- GitHub, [https://github.com/Arize-ai/openinference](https://github.com/Arize-ai/openinference)  
16. Tracing Concepts \- Arize AX Docs, [https://arize.com/docs/ax/instrument/what-are-traces](https://arize.com/docs/ax/instrument/what-are-traces)  
17. (PDF) MALBO: Optimizing LLM-Based Multi-Agent Teams via Multi-Objective Bayesian Optimization \- ResearchGate, [https://www.researchgate.net/publication/397700667\_MALBO\_Optimizing\_LLM-Based\_Multi-Agent\_Teams\_via\_Multi-Objective\_Bayesian\_Optimization](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/397700667_MALBO_Optimizing_LLM-Based_Multi-Agent_Teams_via_Multi-Objective_Bayesian_Optimization)  
18. ODAR: Principled Adaptive Routing for LLM Reasoning via Active Inference \- arXiv, [https://arxiv.org/html/2602.23681v1](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.23681v1)  
19. Adaptive Reasoning Depth in AI Agent Systems: When to Think Hard vs Think Fast \- Zylos, [https://zylos.ai/research/2026-04-13-adaptive-reasoning-depth-ai-agent-systems/](https://zylos.ai/research/2026-04-13-adaptive-reasoning-depth-ai-agent-systems/)  
20. Translating LLM Telemetry Between OpenInference and OTel GenAI with Rust, [https://dev.to/mukundakatta/translating-llm-telemetry-between-openinference-and-otel-genai-with-rust-2bf7](https://dev.to/mukundakatta/translating-llm-telemetry-between-openinference-and-otel-genai-with-rust-2bf7)  
21. Self-Evolving Agents: Three Frameworks That Let Your AI Improve Itself \- Softmax Data, [https://softmaxdata.com/blog/evo/](https://softmaxdata.com/blog/evo/)  
22. How to A/B Test AI Agents With a Bayesian Model \- Parloa, [https://www.parloa.com/labs/research/ai-agent-testing/](https://www.parloa.com/labs/research/ai-agent-testing/)  
23. LLM Based Bayesian Optimization for Prompt Search \- arXiv, [https://arxiv.org/html/2510.04384v1](https://arxiv.org/html/2510.04384v1)  
24. Sequential Probability Ratio Test for AI Products \- Patronus AI, [https://www.patronus.ai/blog/sequential-probability-ratio-test-for-ai-products](https://www.patronus.ai/blog/sequential-probability-ratio-test-for-ai-products)  
25. Sequential Consensus for Multi-Agent LLM Debates: A Wald-SPRT compute governor with calibration-based failure detection \- arXiv, [https://arxiv.org/html/2605.19193v1](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.19193v1)  
26. Sequential Probability Ratio Test: SPRT and Mixture SPRT (mSPRT) | by Carey Chou, [https://medium.com/@carey.chou/sequential-probability-ratio-test-sprt-and-mixture-sprt-msprt-d2a6ef85ff77](https://medium.com/@carey.chou/sequential-probability-ratio-test-sprt-and-mixture-sprt-msprt-d2a6ef85ff77)  
27. Eco-Prompt Engineering \- GI Digital Library, [https://dl.gi.de/bitstreams/95468c19-7415-479d-9d50-3924f994680b/download](https://dl.gi.de/bitstreams/95468c19-7415-479d-9d50-3924f994680b/download)  
28. GENESIS — Cross-Domain AI Optimization Framework | SimuPro, [https://simupro.nl/guides/genesis-cross-domain-ai-optimization-framework/](https://simupro.nl/guides/genesis-cross-domain-ai-optimization-framework/)  
29. Daily Papers \- Hugging Face, [https://huggingface.co/papers?q=shortcut-aware%20difficulty%20framework](https://huggingface.co/papers?q=shortcut-aware+difficulty+framework)  
30. Towards Adaptive, Scalable, and Robust Coordination of LLM Agents: A Dynamic Ad-Hoc Networking Perspective \- arXiv, [https://arxiv.org/html/2602.08009v1](https://arxiv.org/html/2602.08009v1)  
31. Bayesian Orchestration of Multi-LLM Agents for Cost-Aware Sequential Decision-Making, [https://arxiv.org/html/2601.01522v1](https://arxiv.org/html/2601.01522v1)  
32. \[2606.04465\] SePO: Self-Evolving Prompt Agent for System Prompt Optimization \- arXiv, [https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04465](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.04465)  
33. SePO: Self-Evolving Prompt Agent for System Prompt Optimization \- arXiv, [https://arxiv.org/html/2606.04465v1](https://arxiv.org/html/2606.04465v1)  
34. Paper page \- SePO: Self-Evolving Prompt Agent for System Prompt Optimization, [https://huggingface.co/papers/2606.04465](https://huggingface.co/papers/2606.04465)

36. Tutorial-Solution-Optimization.ipynb \- Colab, [https://colab.research.google.com/github/zou-group/TextGrad/blob/main/examples/notebooks/Tutorial-Solution-Optimization.ipynb](https://colab.research.google.com/github/zou-group/TextGrad/blob/main/examples/notebooks/Tutorial-Solution-Optimization.ipynb)
